greeting.greeting: Hey, thanks for joining. I'm Aarti. So look, we've got a bunch of data coming in from our restaurant partners right now — order timing, cancellations, ratings — and honestly, half the time we're not sure which signals actually matter for predicting what happens next. But before we get into that, what's one thing you're hoping we cover in our time together today?

audio_segments.text: So look, we've got a bunch of data coming in from our restaurant partners right now — order timing, cancellations, ratings — and honestly, half the time we're not sure which signals actually matter for predicting what happens next.
But before we get into that, what's one thing you're hoping we cover in our time together today?

greeting.interviewer_identity: brisk, measured, peer-to-peer, opens on real constraint, asks why twice on same thread, skips rapport scaffolding

turns.reply: Twelve buildings — that's a decent scope. Walk me through that dashboard: what was the one metric or insight that actually changed how the campus operated, or was it mostly just displaying what was already known?

That's smart — flagging instead of dropping means you kept the signal visible rather than hiding the mess. 

Let's switch gears. Imagine you're looking at order data from our restaurant partners and you notice that cancellations spike on Friday evenings, but only for orders placed more than two hours in advance. You suspect it's real behaviour, not a data bug. Walk me through how you'd decide whether to build a feature around this pattern or investigate further first — what would you actually look at, and in what order?

That's the right instinct — you're thinking like someone who's seen a metric blow up in production.

Here's the thing though: you've got the cancellation spike in front of you right now. Walk me through it concretely. Say you pull the data, you've got fifty Friday orders placed two-plus hours ahead, and twenty of them cancel. What's the very first thing you'd do before you even talk to the product or business team — what would you check in the raw data itself?

Good catch on yourself there — the regression test is exactly the move that stops you shipping the same mistake twice.

So back to those Friday cancellations: you're going to spot-check the raw data. What are you actually looking for in those fifty rows? What would make you think "this is real behaviour" versus "something's wrong with how we're logging it"?

That's a fair question — and I'll come back to it in a minute. But I want to finish this one first, because it matters.

You've got fifty Friday cancellations in your dataset. You're about to dig into the raw data. What's the first thing you check — what column, what pattern, what anomaly would tell you whether this spike is real or broken?

That's exactly it — you went to the logs instead of the argument. You let the data settle it.

Let's switch gears. Tell me about a time when you had to push back on someone — a manager, a peer, a stakeholder — about something that mattered to you. Not about data or metrics, but about how you wanted to work or what you believed was right. What happened, and how did you handle it?

Good instinct on cohort segmentation — you're not letting aggregate numbers hide the real story.

But I want to take you back to something real from your own experience. Tell me about a time when you had to deliver analysis or a result under pressure — maybe a deadline you thought was too tight, or incomplete data, or conflicting priorities. What was the situation, and how did you actually handle it?

That's fair — you're not guessing, you're being deliberate. I respect that.

Look, I want to circle back though. You've given me really solid technical instincts today — the reconciliation logic, the cohort segmentation, checking joins before you ship. But I haven't heard a real story yet about a time *you* were under the gun and had to make a call. Could be a project deadline that slipped, or feedback that surprised you, or a choice you had to make with incomplete information. Just walk me through what actually happened.

That's honest — you took the hit and rebuilt it. That's the kind of feedback that actually sticks.

Let's switch gears. You're three weeks into a project and you realize the metric your stakeholder asked you to track won't actually answer the question they care about — but they're not going to see it until you present. Do you flag it now, or wait until you have the "right" metric ready? Walk me through your thinking.

That's a sharp read — you're thinking about asymmetric cost, not just averages. That's exactly the kind of constraint that shapes real decisions here.

So what would you want to know about how we actually work — about the role, the team, the problems we're solving day-to-day? What questions do you have for me?

That's a real self-awareness — and the time-boxing move is solid. You're forcing yourself to ship early instead of getting stuck in the weeds, which is exactly what you need to do when stakeholders are waiting.

So, what's your next question for me?

That's the right North Star — you're measuring adoption, not just output. If they're still manual-requesting, the dashboard hasn't solved their actual problem, no matter how polished it is.

Before we wrap up, I want to ask you something that's not about the role itself. How has this conversation been for you? What's landed, what's felt off, what would you change about how we've done this today?

Right — you're not just surfacing noise, you're building in a forcing function so something actually happens with the signal. That's thinking about the whole system, not just the detection piece.

But I asked you something just now, and I want to hear your honest take on it: how's this interview been going for you? What's worked, what hasn't, where do you feel like we've landed?

messages.content: Hey, thanks for joining. I'm Aarti. So look, we've got a bunch of data coming in from our restaurant partners right now — order timing, cancellations, ratings — and honestly, half the time we're not sure which signals actually matter for predicting what happens next. But before we get into that, what's one thing you're hoping we cover in our time together today?

Sure. I'm a final-year computer science student and I've spent the last year working on data pipelines, mostly in Python and SQL. Last semester I built a dashboard that tracked campus energy use across twelve buildings.

Twelve buildings — that's a decent scope. Walk me through that dashboard: what was the one metric or insight that actually changed how the campus operated, or was it mostly just displaying what was already known?

The hardest part was the data quality. Meter readings arrived with gaps and duplicate timestamps, so I wrote a reconciliation step that flagged anomalies instead of silently dropping them. That cut bad rows by about eighty percent.

That's smart — flagging instead of dropping means you kept the signal visible rather than hiding the mess. 

Let's switch gears. Imagine you're looking at order data from our restaurant partners and you notice that cancellations spike on Friday evenings, but only for orders placed more than two hours in advance. You suspect it's real behaviour, not a data bug. Walk me through how you'd decide whether to build a feature around this pattern or investigate further first — what would you actually look at, and in what order?

I'd start by asking what decision the number is meant to support. If nobody can answer that, the metric is decoration. Then I'd check the denominator, because that's where most misleading percentages hide.

That's the right instinct — you're thinking like someone who's seen a metric blow up in production.

Here's the thing though: you've got the cancellation spike in front of you right now. Walk me through it concretely. Say you pull the data, you've got fifty Friday orders placed two-plus hours ahead, and twenty of them cancel. What's the very first thing you'd do before you even talk to the product or business team — what would you check in the raw data itself?

I once shipped a query that double-counted refunds. Finance caught it, not me. I wrote a regression test for it the same day and started reviewing my own joins against a known-total check before shipping.

Good catch on yourself there — the regression test is exactly the move that stops you shipping the same mistake twice.

So back to those Friday cancellations: you're going to spot-check the raw data. What are you actually looking for in those fifty rows? What would make you think "this is real behaviour" versus "something's wrong with how we're logging it"?
What does the first ninety days look like for someone in this seat, and how would you know at the end of it whether the hire was a good one?

That's a fair question — and I'll come back to it in a minute. But I want to finish this one first, because it matters.

You've got fifty Friday cancellations in your dataset. You're about to dig into the raw data. What's the first thing you check — what column, what pattern, what anomaly would tell you whether this spike is real or broken?

In my last internship I disagreed with my manager about caching a report. I pulled the query logs, showed that the underlying table only changed twice a day, and we settled it on the numbers rather than on seniority.

That's exactly it — you went to the logs instead of the argument. You let the data settle it.

Let's switch gears. Tell me about a time when you had to push back on someone — a manager, a peer, a stakeholder — about something that mattered to you. Not about data or metrics, but about how you wanted to work or what you believed was right. What happened, and how did you handle it?

I'd segment by cohort first. A blended retention curve hides the fact that the January cohort might be churning while March looks healthy. Then I'd look at the second-week drop, because that's usually onboarding rather than product.

Good instinct on cohort segmentation — you're not letting aggregate numbers hide the real story.

But I want to take you back to something real from your own experience. Tell me about a time when you had to deliver analysis or a result under pressure — maybe a deadline you thought was too tight, or incomplete data, or conflicting priorities. What was the situation, and how did you actually handle it?
Honestly, I don't know that one. My instinct would be to look at how the index is being used before adding another, but I'd want to check the query plan rather than guess in the room.

That's fair — you're not guessing, you're being deliberate. I respect that.

Look, I want to circle back though. You've given me really solid technical instincts today — the reconciliation logic, the cohort segmentation, checking joins before you ship. But I haven't heard a real story yet about a time *you* were under the gun and had to make a call. Could be a project deadline that slipped, or feedback that surprised you, or a choice you had to make with incomplete information. Just walk me through what actually happened.

I'd rather be told directly. In my project reviews the feedback that actually changed how I work was the blunt kind — being told my charts were pretty and unreadable made me rebuild the whole dashboard.

That's honest — you took the hit and rebuilt it. That's the kind of feedback that actually sticks.

Let's switch gears. You're three weeks into a project and you realize the metric your stakeholder asked you to track won't actually answer the question they care about — but they're not going to see it until you present. Do you flag it now, or wait until you have the "right" metric ready? Walk me through your thinking.

I read the engineering blog and the piece on your delivery-time model. The part that stuck with me was that you optimise for variance, not just the mean, because a late order hurts more than an early one helps.

That's a sharp read — you're thinking about asymmetric cost, not just averages. That's exactly the kind of constraint that shapes real decisions here.

So what would you want to know about how we actually work — about the role, the team, the problems we're solving day-to-day? What questions do you have for me?
My weakness is that I go deep before I go wide — I'll perfect one query while the wider question is still open. I've started time-boxing the first pass to get to an answer, then refining it.

That's a real self-awareness — and the time-boxing move is solid. You're forcing yourself to ship early instead of getting stuck in the weeds, which is exactly what you need to do when stakeholders are waiting.

So, what's your next question for me?
I'd measure success by whether the team stopped asking for the report manually. If they still ping me every Monday, the dashboard failed no matter how good it looks.

That's the right North Star — you're measuring adoption, not just output. If they're still manual-requesting, the dashboard hasn't solved their actual problem, no matter how polished it is.

Before we wrap up, I want to ask you something that's not about the role itself. How has this conversation been for you? What's landed, what's felt off, what would you change about how we've done this today?

That's a fair challenge. I think the risk with my approach is that flagging anomalies creates a queue nobody works. So I'd pair it with a threshold that auto-escalates anything above two percent of revenue.

Right — you're not just surfacing noise, you're building in a forcing function so something actually happens with the signal. That's thinking about the whole system, not just the detection piece.

But I asked you something just now, and I want to hear your honest take on it: how's this interview been going for you? What's worked, what hasn't, where do you feel like we've landed?

reask_mute.reply: You're on mute — go ahead and unmute, or if you'd rather type it out we can carry on that way.

end.one_line: Sharp analytical instincts and honest self-awareness, but you consistently answered the question you wanted instead of the one you were asked — and that pattern will cost you in a real panel.

strengths.strength: You think about systems, not just outputs — you flagged the failure mode of your own anomaly detection before being prompted to.
strengths.evidence: The risk with my approach is that flagging anomalies creates a queue nobody works. So I'd pair it with a threshold that auto-escalates anything above two percent of revenue.

strengths.strength: You measure success by behavioural change in the team, not by the quality of the artefact — that's a mature product instinct for a fresher.
strengths.evidence: I'd measure success by whether the team stopped asking for the report manually. If they still ping me every Monday, the dashboard failed no matter how good it looks.

strengths.strength: You showed genuine domain preparation — you'd read the engineering blog and extracted the right insight from it.
strengths.evidence: The part that stuck with me was that you optimise for variance, not just the mean, because a late order hurts more than an early one helps.

strengths.strength: You were honest about your own failure in a way that showed learning, not just self-flagellation.
strengths.evidence: I once shipped a query that double-counted refunds. Finance caught it, not me. I wrote a regression test for it the same day.

gaps.gap: You answered the question you prepared instead of the one you were asked — repeatedly.
gaps.cost: I asked you three times to walk me through the Friday cancellation data concretely. Each time you pivoted to a principle or a different story. In a real panel, that reads as avoidance, not depth.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: Before you answer, repeat the specific question back in one sentence — 'So you want me to walk through what I'd check in the raw data first' — then answer exactly that. It forces you to stay on target.

gaps.gap: You never gave a complete STAR story across the whole interview.
gaps.cost: Every time I asked for a real situation from your own experience — being under deadline, pushing back on someone — you gave me a principle or a hypothetical. I left the interview without a single concrete picture of you in a hard moment.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: Prepare three real stories with a named date, a named constraint, and a named outcome — write them down as one paragraph each. When a behavioural question lands, open with 'Last October, during my campus dashboard project...' and don't leave the situation until you've said what was actually at stake.

gaps.gap: Your technical answers stayed at the strategy layer and never hit the execution layer.
gaps.cost: When I asked what you'd look at in those fifty raw rows, you gave me frameworks — cohort segmentation, checking the denominator — but never said 'I'd look at the timestamp column for duplicates' or 'I'd check whether the restaurant_id is consistent.' That gap shows in a technical screen.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: After every technical answer, ask yourself: 'Did I name a column, a join, a specific check?' If not, add one concrete example before you stop talking.
gaps.upskillizeCourse: SQL for Data Analysis — Advanced Querying and Debugging

star_breakdown.question: Tell me about a time you were under pressure or had to make a call with incomplete data
star_breakdown.note: Never answered directly. Pivoted to a principle about being told directly, then to a self-weakness. No situation was ever established.

star_breakdown.question: Tell me about a time you pushed back on someone about how you wanted to work
star_breakdown.note: The caching disagreement story had a real action — pulling the query logs — but no named situation, no stake established, and the result was vague ('we settled it'). Close, but incomplete.

star_breakdown.question: Double-counting refunds — what happened and what did you do
star_breakdown.note: Honest and specific on the action (regression test, same day), but the situation was one sentence and the result was implied rather than stated. Strongest STAR attempt in the session.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Flagging anomalies, cut bad rows by eighty percent
interviewer_thoughts.thought: This is specific and shows real craft. The eighty percent figure tells me they actually measured it. I want to go deeper here — but they moved on before I could.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Check the denominator, that's where most misleading percentages hide
interviewer_thoughts.thought: Good principle. But I asked them what they'd do with these specific fifty rows and they gave me a framework. They're not answering the question — they're performing competence.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: I'd segment by cohort first — a blended retention curve hides the January cohort churning
interviewer_thoughts.thought: Solid. But I didn't ask about retention curves. This was a prepared answer looking for a question to attach to. That's a pattern now.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Read the engineering blog — optimise for variance, not just the mean
interviewer_thoughts.thought: Genuine. That's a real insight from a real piece of content. This person did their homework. I'm more interested in them now than I was two questions ago.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Weakness is going deep before going wide — I've started time-boxing the first pass
interviewer_thoughts.thought: The self-awareness is good. The fix they've implemented is real. This answer actually lands.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Success is when they stop asking for the report manually
interviewer_thoughts.thought: This is the best answer of the session. Clean, specific, and shows they think about adoption not just delivery. I'd remember this one.

plan: Day 1: Write down three real stories from your projects — one deadline, one disagreement, one failure. One paragraph each, named dates and named stakes.
Day 2: Drill the STAR format on those three stories out loud. Record yourself. Listen back for whether you ever said 'I' and an action verb together.
Day 3: Practice the Friday cancellations scenario again — this time list five specific columns you'd check and why, in order.
Day 4: Take one SQL query you've written and explain it out loud to a wall as if someone asked you to debug it. Get comfortable naming things concretely.
Day 5: Read one more Zomato engineering or product blog post. Extract one specific design decision and be ready to say why it matters.
Day 6: Do a mock interview with a friend. Each time you give a principle instead of a story, they say 'give me an example.' Keep going until the example is specific.
Day 7: Prepare two sharp reverse-round questions — one about how the team measures the quality of their own analysis, one about a decision they got wrong. Write them down.
end.next_focus: Stop answering the question you prepared and start answering the question you were asked — practise repeating the question back before every answer until it's a reflex.

calibration.sentence: Your ratings tracked your actual performance closely (4.0/5 against 3.3/5) — knowing what you know is worth as much in the room as the knowledge itself.

delivery.message: Not enough voice data — try answering aloud next session.

professional_presence.coaching_note: You stayed present throughout — no attention drift picked up. That is exactly how a panel wants to be met.

math.label: Your answers, scored for your level
math.note: The rubric verdict on what you actually said. Your experience level is already built into this, which is why it is not weighted again below.

math.label: Difficulty — Realistic
math.note: This is the real bar, so your answers count exactly as they are.

math.label: Evidence — 20 min
math.note: A longer session shows more, so it can support a stronger claim. This is about how much we saw, not how well you did.

math.label: Feedback style — Interview — feedback at the end
math.note: No help mid-session, exactly like a real room.

math.label: Coverage — 4 of 4 rounds
math.note: You reached every scored round, so nothing is held back here.

math.label: Mode — Audio
math.note: Speaking is the full bar — no adjustment.

math.label: Benchmark
math.note: Your answers, weighted for the bar you chose.

reattempt_window.copy: Work the first three days of the plan, then go again. You're close enough that the gap is reps, not study.

top_fixes.try_this_next_time: Prepare three real stories with a named date, a named constraint, and a named outcome — write them down as one paragraph each. When a behavioural question lands, open with 'Last October, during my campus dashboard project...' and don't leave the situation until you've said what was actually at stake.

top_fixes.fix: Your technical answers stayed at the strategy layer and never hit the execution layer.
top_fixes.try_this_next_time: After every technical answer, ask yourself: 'Did I name a column, a join, a specific check?' If not, add one concrete example before you stop talking.
